The experiments on three popular public gait datasets, i.e., CASIA-B, OUMVLP, and Gait3D, demonstrate that the proposed method can provide substantial improvements over the current state-of-the-art methods.

The terms 'conditions' and 'wearing coats' are related as the context discusses different carrying conditions and the change in silhouette appearance based on different clothing conditions.

The terms 'conditions' and 'intra-class variance' are related as different conditions greatly change the silhouette appearance of the same person, leading to a higher intra-class variance compared to inter-class variance.

The terms 'conditions' and 'external factors' are related as the question of learning more robust features adaptively for each person under the influence of various external factors is raised in the context.
The terms 'Local Motion Patterns' and 'Gait' are related as the discussion focuses on observing unique representative motion patterns for each body part in a gait sequence.
The terms 'Gait' and 'body parts' are related as gait is described as the coordinated movement of body parts in the context.

The terms 'Local Conv-Mixing Block (LCMB)' and 'Global Motion Patterns Aggregator (GMPA)' are related as both are mentioned as well-designed components used in encoding features and aggregating motion patterns, respectively.
The LCMB is proposed to extract representative local motion patterns that can dynamically model relationships among neighboring pixels and accurately locate key regions.

The GMPA is designed to select discriminative local motion patterns and aggregate them for a robust global representation, being the first to explore the potential of a self-attention model in this task.
Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, outperforming the SOTA method on various datasets such as CASIA-B, OUMVLP, and Gait3D.
Gait recognition approaches are categorized into model-based and appearance-based approaches in the field of gait recognition.

Model-based approaches attempt to explicitly fit human pose structures to images. However, the predefined points are empirically designed and limited by the inaccuracy of estimation results for low-quality images. Therefore, the model-based methods are generally inferior to appearance-based methods in performance.

Appearance-based approaches are the mainstream frameworks for gait and have benefited from the quick growth of deep learning. They can be roughly divided into three classes, namely template-based, Mean-based, and Global Motion Pattern Aggregator (GMPA) module.

The Global Motion Pattern Aggregator (GMPA) module is critical to accurately locate the discriminative parts and obtain representative local motion patterns under the interference of various external factors.
Previous gait-based approaches simply use convolutional networks with non-linear activation to model the dynamic movements.

The global motion patterns aggregator (GMPA) is a new instantiation of the global action modeling framework for gait recognition.
The GMPA utilizes an attention mechanism to aggregate query-specific global motion patterns of each query location.
The GMPA selects a preset number of distinguished local motion patterns and models the global movement patterns of the corresponding part.
The GMPA generates global low-order motion patterns by squeezing variable local motion patterns and constructing an attention map in the temporal dimension.
The global low-order motion patterns are generated by local motion patterns and attention maps, and are expressed through matrix multiplication.

CASIA-B and OUMVLP are both gait datasets mentioned in the context. CASIA-B contains 124 subjects with 3 different walking conditions, while OUMVLP has the largest number of sequences among public gait datasets.
CASIA-B dataset includes 3 different walking conditions: normal walking (NM), walking with bags (BG), and walking wearing a coat (CL).
OUMVLP dataset consists of 2 different walking sequences ('00-01').
OUMVLP dataset has 14 different camera viewpoints uniformly distributed in [0°, 90°] and [180°, 270°].
